title: Listening - Part 3: Getting Around Town
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a way of travelling around town they use regularly. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for choosing this way to travel. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a way of travelling around town they use regularly. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for choosing this way to travel. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about travelling around their town.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I used to catch a bus to work, although it was only a fairly short distance. The problem was that the road into the centre becomes completely blocked on weekday mornings. Some days I arrived early, and on others I was apologising to my manager. [19]I started cycling because I can use a separate path and know exactly how long the journey will take, rather than depending on traffic conditions.[/19] I enjoy getting fresh air as well, but being consistently on time is the reason I haven't gone back to the bus.||Speaker 2 Man: My family has two cars, and until recently I used one whenever I wanted to go into town, even for short journeys. Then our school carried out a project on local air quality, measuring pollution near busy roads. [20]I began taking the tram because it seemed wrong to add another car to the traffic when public transport could take me to the same places with less environmental impact.[/20] It sometimes means waiting a few minutes, but I can read on the journey and no longer have to find a parking space.||Speaker 3 Woman: I recently stopped buying monthly bus tickets and began walking most places in town. People assume I'm doing it to get fit, and I admit my legs have become stronger. However, that was not how the decision began. [21]After paying rent and food costs, I realised public transport was using a large part of my weekly budget, whereas walking to college and the shops costs nothing.[/21] When it's pouring with rain, I still take the bus, but usually I prefer to keep the money for other things.||Speaker 4 Man: I run a small stall at weekend markets, selling homemade bread and cakes. At first I carried everything on the bus, which was difficult and sometimes ended with damaged cakes before I even arrived. I didn't want the cost of a car. [22]I bought an electric cargo bike because its large box lets me carry trays, signs and equipment safely to the market without struggling with several bags.[/22] It also attracts attention when I arrive, so people often come over to see what I'm selling.||Speaker 5 Woman: I have evening classes twice a week, and they finish after dark. The route home is safe enough, but I didn't enjoy waiting alone for a late bus, particularly in winter. A neighbour attends the same college building. [23]We now share a taxi home after class because travelling together feels much more pleasant than making the late journey by myself.[/23] It costs a little more than the bus, but split between us it is manageable, and our conversations on the way home are often the best part of the evening.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I used to catch a bus to work, although it was only a fairly short distance. The problem was that the road into the centre becomes completely blocked on weekday mornings. Some days I arrived early, and on others I was apologising to my manager. I started cycling because I can use a separate path and know exactly how long the journey will take, rather than depending on traffic conditions. I enjoy getting fresh air as well, but being consistently on time is the reason I haven't gone back to the bus.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: My family has two cars, and until recently I used one whenever I wanted to go into town, even for short journeys. Then our school carried out a project on local air quality, measuring pollution near busy roads. I began taking the tram because it seemed wrong to add another car to the traffic when public transport could take me to the same places with less environmental impact. It sometimes means waiting a few minutes, but I can read on the journey and no longer have to find a parking space.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I recently stopped buying monthly bus tickets and began walking most places in town. People assume I'm doing it to get fit, and I admit my legs have become stronger. However, that was not how the decision began. After paying rent and food costs, I realised public transport was using a large part of my weekly budget, whereas walking to college and the shops costs nothing. When it's pouring with rain, I still take the bus, but usually I prefer to keep the money for other things.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I run a small stall at weekend markets, selling homemade bread and cakes. At first I carried everything on the bus, which was difficult and sometimes ended with damaged cakes before I even arrived. I didn't want the cost of a car. I bought an electric cargo bike because its large box lets me carry trays, signs and equipment safely to the market without struggling with several bags. It also attracts attention when I arrive, so people often come over to see what I'm selling.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I have evening classes twice a week, and they finish after dark. The route home is safe enough, but I didn't enjoy waiting alone for a late bus, particularly in winter. A neighbour attends the same college building. We now share a taxi home after class because travelling together feels much more pleasant than making the late journey by myself. It costs a little more than the bus, but split between us it is manageable, and our conversations on the way home are often the best part of the evening.

options.text: to see more of the town

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Cycling gives the speaker a predictable journey time and prevents lateness caused by traffic (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker switched to the tram after becoming concerned about pollution caused by cars (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker walks primarily because it avoids the cost of frequent bus travel (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The electric cargo bike allows the speaker to transport products and stall equipment safely (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker shares a taxi because she prefers not to make a late journey alone (evidence [23]).
